column1: # Review log for cited sources — the unified resolution log.
#   1. You ADD a new entry to src/data/sources.ts.
#   2. You RESOLVE an audit issue (audit:link or audit:report).
#   3. You DO A PERIODIC REVIEW and verify a citation still backs the claim.
# The audit makes the level of human involvement transparent rather than
# absent: AI assistance is allowed and useful, especially during active
# development; the row's `kind` column records honestly what kind of
# Format: id \t YYYY-MM-DD \t reviewer \t kind \t notes (tab-separated)
# `id` is the stable source slug from src/data/sources.ts — the outer key
# `state-dor-ca`, `state-snap-tx`). Keying by id rather than URL means
# review history follows the source through URL changes.
# `kind` records what kind of verification happened:
#   - `human` — eyes-on-source, no AI assistance. Use this only when you
#               personally read the destination and confirmed the cited
#               figures. Required for community-submitted reviews via the
#   - `ai`    — AI was involved in proposing, extracting, or refreshing
#               this entry. Whatever human review followed (a glance, a
#               careful read, none) is self-reported and unverifiable, so
#               the kind doesn't try to subdivide. Be honest: if you
#   - `verified-bot-blocked` — confirmation that a URL which curl reports
#               as bot-blocked (typically 999) loads in a real browser.
#               Lighter bar than `human`: you only need to confirm the
#               URL is reachable, not that the destination still cites
#               the claim. Suppresses that source from the broken-citation
#               queue for 30 DAYS — deliberately tighter than the
#               staleness cadence because bot-blocked URLs lose nightly
#               automated reachability checks, so a human "this loads"
#               verification is the only liveness signal. Does NOT reset
#               the staleness clock (that still measures from your last
#               full `human`/`ai` review). Use for legitimate sites with
#               anti-bot defenses (LinkedIn, some state agency pages).
# still accepted by the parser for backwards compatibility — both fold
# into `ai` at parse time. New rows should use `ai`.
# Reviewer should be your GitHub username (no @ prefix). Renders as a
# All rows in this file are 5-column. The pre-kind-column 4-field
# format is no longer present in this file — the four legacy rows that
# existed under that schema were faithfully padded to 5 columns
# (kind=human, which is how they parsed before) during the
# 2026-05-03 rotation. The pre-rotation file is preserved verbatim at
#   2. Rewrite reviewed.tsv with all rows in the new schema
#      verifies no row disappears: missing rows must be present in some
#      archive/*.tsv. Schema migrations are explicit, recorded, and
#      can't be used to silently rewrite history.

column5: notes
HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1, Change 4 (Nov 2013). Confirmed the landing page lists the handbook + chapters; Ch. 3 covers occupancy. Replaces the 404'd /sites/dfiles/OCHCO/documents/4350.3.pdf citation.

KFF moved the annual survey to a year-stable series archive. Confirmed the page links to the latest (2025) report with the figures we anchor employer health-insurance employee-share to. Replaces the 404'd /health-costs/report/employer-health-benefits-annual-survey/.
Care.com renamed the cost-of-care landing page. Confirmed the new URL hosts the current Cost of Care Report with per-metro childcare cost figures. Replaces the 404'd /c/cost-of-childcare/.

Child Care Aware retired the /state-fact-sheets/ page. The 2024 Price & Supply report at /price-landscape24/ has an interactive state selector with per-state child-care price breakdowns — same content territory we cited the fact sheets for. Year-stamped slug; staleness audit will surface it on the next cycle.

column1: # 2026-05-03: SS_WAGE_BASE correction PR — human verification of the corrected value.

column5: Human-verified against the SSA Contribution and Benefit Base table (1937-2026). The codebase had SS_WAGE_BASE = 181000, which matches no year — the 2025 row shows 176,100 and the 2026 row shows 184,500. The comment referenced "2025 wage base used through 2026 SS adjustments," which was a confused gloss. Corrected the constant to 184,500 (TY2026 cap) and rewrote the comment to reflect actual SSA semantics. Supersedes the 2026-05-02 ai backfill row above.

BLS CEX 2023-2024 two-year-average regional and divisional expenditure tables (Tables 1800 + 2700). User downloaded the official xlsx files from bls.gov/cex/tables/geographic/mean.htm and provided them; AI parsed via openpyxl, walked the row layout (label / Mean / SE / RSE), extracted Mean values for 15 schema line items per region (4) and division (9), and populated the constants in src/data/cex.ts. Composite-field math (utilitiesElectricGas = naturalGas + electricity + fuelOil; healthcareOOP = medicalServices + drugs + medicalSupplies) inlined as comments next to each cell so a reader can audit the arithmetic. Any subsequent eyes-on-source verification will be recorded as a separate kind=human row.

BLS CEX 2024 single-year Table 1101 (Quintiles of income before taxes). User mirrored the official xlsx into audit/data-sources/bls-cex/2024/cu-income-quintiles-before-taxes-2024.xlsx; AI parsed via openpyxl and extracted Mean values for 15 schema line items across 5 quintiles plus the 4 quintile-floor income thresholds (q2=$29,932, q3=$57,452, q4=$94,511, q5=$155,925) into NATIONAL_QUINTILE_SPENDING and QUINTILE_THRESHOLDS_2024 in src/data/cex.ts. Composite-field math (utilitiesElectricGas = naturalGas + electricity + fuelOil; healthcareOOP = medicalServices + drugs + medicalSupplies) inlined as comments next to each cell. Education non-monotonicity (q1 > q2) flagged in the docstring as a real BLS quirk, not a data error. Vintage asymmetry with the geo tables (2024 single-year vs 2023-2024 two-year-average) documented inline. Any subsequent eyes-on-source verification will be recorded as a separate kind=human row.

Scope expanded — same source, now backs Region + Division + MSA. Pulled 22 MSA tables from audit/data-sources/bls-cex/2023-2024/cu-msa-{northeast,midwest,south,west}-2-year-average-2023-2024.xlsx (Tables 3004 / 3015 / 3024 / 3033) via openpyxl. MSA tables publish ~12 of 15 schema line items; utilitiesElectricGas, utilitiesWaterPublic, and healthcareOOP are not broken out at MSA level (BLS publishes them as combined "Utilities, fuels, and public services" and "Healthcare" rolled-up rows), so those fields stay undefined per MSA and the blend falls through to division. Gasoline at MSA level is "Gasoline and other fuels" (slightly broader than the region/division "Gasoline" denominator); minor inflation noted in the BLSMSA docstring. Source label updated from "Region & Division" to "Region, Division & MSA".

BLS CEX 2024 single-year Table 1800 (Region of residence — Mean income before taxes). New source split off from bls-cex-income-quintiles-2024 because Table 1800 is a different table than Table 1101 even though both are BLS CEX 2024 tables; Copilot review on PR #187 flagged the citation as imprecise. Backs REGION_MEAN_HHI_2024_BEFORE_TAX in src/data/cex.ts (Northeast $115,770 / Midwest $97,104 / South $93,814 / West $120,365), used by the IncomePosition thermometer regional-mean marker. User mirrored the xlsx into audit/data-sources/bls-cex/2024/cu-region-1-year-average-2024.xlsx; AI parsed via openpyxl and extracted the "Income before taxes / Mean" row.

BLS CEX 2024 single-year Table 1400 (Size of consumer unit). New source split off from the broader bls-cex-income-quintiles-2024 family because Table 1400 is its own published cross-tab. User mirrored the official xlsx into audit/data-sources/bls-cex/2024/cu-size-2024.xlsx; AI parsed via openpyxl and extracted Mean values for 15 schema line items across the 1-person / 2-person / 3-person / 4-person / 5+ person columns plus the All-CU baseline column into SIZE_ALLCU_SPENDING and SIZE_BASELINE_ALLCU in src/data/cex.ts. Composite-field math (utilitiesElectricGas = naturalGas + electricity + fuelOil; healthcareOOP = medicalServices + drugs + medicalSupplies; householdOperations = personalServices + otherHouseholdExpenses) inlined as comments next to each cell. Backs the new size factor in blendCexSpending (issue #128) — closes the systematic bias of using "average CU" (~2.5 person) values regardless of actual household size. Cross-vintage drift (single-year 1400 vs two-year geo tables) measured at <4% per line, asserted by cex.test.ts.

Scope expanded — same source, additional sublines pulled. Added cellularService, lifeInsurance, vehicleInsurance, vehicleMaintRepair, alcohol, otherLodging, and pets to NATIONAL_QUINTILE_SPENDING in src/data/cex.ts. Each is a discrete subline of an existing parent we already pulled (cellular under "Telephone services", life insurance under "Personal insurance and pensions", etc.). Pulled via openpyxl from the same audit/data-sources/bls-cex/2024/cu-income-quintiles-before-taxes-2024.xlsx mirror. Note: pensions sub-line of "Personal insurance and pensions" intentionally NOT pulled — it overlaps with FICA (already modeled) and pre-tax retirement contributions (roadmap #4).

Scope expanded — additional sublines pulled. Added cellularService, lifeInsurance, vehicleInsurance, vehicleMaintRepair, alcohol, otherLodging, and pets to REGION_ALLCU_SPENDING and DIVISION_ALLCU_SPENDING in src/data/cex.ts. MSA tables (3001-3033) do not publish these sublines (only ~48 aggregate rows per MSA), so MSA_ALLCU_SPENDING leaves the new keys undefined and the blend falls through to division — same pattern used by existing rolled-up lines (utilitiesElectricGas, healthcareOOP).

Scope expanded — additional sublines pulled. Added cellularService, lifeInsurance, vehicleInsurance, vehicleMaintRepair, alcohol, otherLodging, and pets to SIZE_ALLCU_SPENDING and SIZE_BASELINE_ALLCU in src/data/cex.ts. Cross-vintage drift bound (vs the 2-year geo baseline) widened <4% → <6% to accommodate vehicleInsurance ~5.8% — real economic drift, auto-insurance premiums rose sharply in 2024.

Added new source for state-level insurance data (auto / homeowners / renters). III aggregates NAIC state-level avg expenditure data; one entry covers all 50 states with state context passed at render time per the hybrid rollup pattern (same as STATE_TAX_SOURCE for tax DORs). Wired into EXPENSE_SOURCE descriptions for Renters insurance, Homeowners insurance (Roadmap #13 placeholder). Per-cell data wiring to follow.

Added new source for residential broadband price benchmarks. The FCC Urban Rate Survey publishes residential broadband pricing the FCC uses for high-cost program purposes; serves as the planned source for the Home internet leaf, which is currently a $70/mo flat placeholder. Per-cell value wiring to follow.

Added EIA Electric Power Monthly Table 5.6.A (Average Price of Electricity by State, residential). Existing eia-residential source covers consumption; this is the price-by-state companion that augments utilitiesElectricGas with state-level ¢/kWh signal (3× spread HI vs WA). Source added; per-cell wiring to follow.

Added NCES Digest of Education Statistics Table 205.50 (Private elementary and secondary school tuition). Planned source for K-12 tuition split when Education leaf is later subdivided into K-12 / College / Supplies. Source added; sub-leaf split to follow.
Added College Board Trends in College Pricing. Planned source for College / 529 sub-leaf when Education is later split. Source added; sub-leaf split to follow.
Added Census ACS B25088 (Median Selected Monthly Owner Costs). Planned baseline source for Mortgage P&I leaf when roadmap #13 lands. Cited in EXPENSE_SOURCE.Mortgage_P_I description.
Added Census ACS B25103 (Median Real Estate Taxes Paid). Planned source for Property tax leaf, paired with Tax Foundation per-state effective rates. Cited in EXPENSE_SOURCE.Property_tax description.

Added DOL National Database of Childcare Prices (NDCP). Planned alternative / cross-check source for Childcare leaf alongside existing care-com-childcare. NDCP publishes county-level child-care prices by age band; can supplement or replace Care.com for finer geographic resolution.
